ayoreo: Ga chi ore doi*Tináiné*, ga*Tináiné*chijna re aja jogadi, ore ijnanguiéni, ga chojninga: ‘Déji de uacajnanguiéni.’
english: And he would say: ‘Here is your lost thing.’

ayoreo: *Queyadi*ujeta yipéjo*queyidai*iñoñaringuei tome Dupade anguresónique ore. Jé uacamóño to uje*yatecãri*Davidé ica. Dupade anguresõre ore adi u. E yuruode to uje Davidé chachai ujuyaquei ome uyoque que. Ujeta Dupade anguresóningo ore isocai yocadi u. Uje yimesacoa ñane ome cuchade.
english: That is how Eri is, he was not ashamed to teach us when he came to visit us, and to remind us of many things from Dupade’s Word, and how that there’s no way we should talk badly about our fellow*ayoréos*. And in the same way he shared other things – My insides should not be ‘hot’ (angry) with my fellow*ayoréos*. I should not accuse my fellow*ayoréos*of doing ugly things. That’s a very wrong thing to do according to Dupade’s Word.

ayoreo: Cojñoque ore iji yocujade chi ore chojninga: “Que yanguretágo Dupade. Arapataque ore gu, acadisórone, Pedro ore. A guérique tagu yoque mu yeru poridie,” ore chojninga. “Yẽco cucaranone. Amo cucarani uje de ti uje umare. Ga yocajéo. A yoquisatígo chise jeta yotique chise de pajei.”
english: Those*cojñone*that were among us used to say: “We don’t believe in*Dupade*. Peter and the others who teach you are liars when they tell about the flood. If we should have rain like they claim it rained back then, we’ll just climb up in the trees. Or we’ll climb a mountain. You see how tall the mountains are? That’s our plan if the water should reach up to our waists.”

ayoreo:
Uje que ore changureta to enga
ore chichaga erámi taja ñane. Ore
chichaga erámi taja tijode ga pénu ome ore. Uje urasade e ore jnome, e ore cho jmíti-jmíti ome
ñane gajnerĩ.
english: On the other hand, these will be the words of the others. They will say: “*Dupade*has arrived!”

ayoreo:
*(Juan 16:22)*Jesute acajnaquéode u yajirigode ome yoque iji San Juan urusarane uje Jesus qué déji jnumi nanique ga chatata dayé adode. Chatata dasagode ome danguresórone uje qué déji jnumi. Chatata anírone cojñone ijmongaquéode ome. Jesute, chamáñaje ujopierãque, mu chudute pijmongaquéode unojo to. Jecute chojningame dacajnaquéode udojo ome danguresórone. Chojninga:  “Yi jnenga, yijiapo ome gaté jne rĩ, enga páyuguhebátiga jne ome uaque. Jé basa ga pininaqué yicoringai tome uaque,” Jesus chojningame danguresórone uje déji jnumi nanique. Mu anguresórone que ore chiraja. Jesus jorane
que ore chiraja uruode quigode go. Ga chi ore cho dabuja ome ñane. Jesus
acadigode ore chojninga: “¿Chimo jóroque daruode quitique?  Uje chi aníri, chi pininaquei jne mu
pujnusiétiga rĩ.” Ga que ore chiraja Jesus acajnaquéode quitique.
english: I pray for the missionaries who live here in our village also that we might learn more and more of*your Words*from their lips. We know that they know your*Word*, but they do not speak our language very well, and only you know all these things,*Yoque Dupade.*

ayoreo:
“Ca apota gapu unejángueraque,
cheque unejángueraque.” Ga gosi pota jeta changureta Dupade, ga ca goto
ajeigaite. Ca potapise dojóraque chequé, dojórique jnani, jnacare acotéraguiji
dejórique. A gosi pota gapu unejángue ore mu e cucha gajnáreque ome ujórique
ayoré. Gajnáreque cuchade.
english: “Don’t desire young women, or the wives of other men.” If a man wants to believe in*Dupade*, he mustn’t sleep with the women who come to him. And he should not lust after other women, and women should not lust after other men and other people’s mates, we shouldn’t lust after them. To do those things is ugly.

ayoreo:
Jé basája ga Dupade chi di jne
enga na yógaja yocao ichai uje que tãratápo gotique rĩ. Enga unojo u uje que qué
pipotiguérique, puru patáringa chisapo yóguiji ne jnumi rĩ.
english: It’s not possible for*Dupade*to abandon us. I can stop obeying his commands. You may stop believing in*Dupade*, but he won’t stop believing in you at all. You could stop following*Dupade*and make the Holy Spirit ashamed of you. We know how a man, or a woman sometimes scolds their relatives. And they feel embarrassed around their relatives and don’t visit them anymore. It’s much like that when you stop following*Dupade*.*Dupade*’s Holy Spirit stays in a corner of you heart and is very sad. He hunches his shoulders from sadness.

ayoreo: *Dijaide – Rincón de Tigre, Bolivia – 1970**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Dijaide acajnaquéode unojo*
english: Dupade would know me. He would know that I considered my good works to be my savior, and that I wasn’t depending on what Jesus Christ did when he sold himself for me.

ayoreo:
Enga gajnui
jec u uje yocajneque Dupade Uruode jnese, mu yocuñane chetaque Dupade Uruode
enga gajnui tu co dusi uje anire gajnarengué ujetiga yójigo yodi aja. Ga adi
jec u uje gosi a pota enga gajnui tu co etocoroi uje etogué enga ueradé tu
uaté. Co etocoroidie ome yodi. Ga gajnui jec u ayoré uje misionéro uje doi
Dupade Uruode enga uté u uje jno Dupade Uruode údi ga chingo Jesus acajnaquéode
aja yocujode ayoré, enga anire chojninga: “Jesuse chingo dedai taja yoque jnese
jeta yijínango aja,” chojninga.
english: *Ecarai**–**Tobité, Boliv**ia – 1970.* *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie**.*

ayoreo:
Enga
yicatecã́ri Dupade ome uaque to, yurasade uaque á. Yutai Dupade ujetiga uaque
uñeque changureta to enga angoninguesã́tigo déji uaque.
english: And for you, my countrymen, I hope that you believe also, and have obedient hearts and open ears.

ayoreo: Mu uacarajáyo uje Dupade pésu dejnai gu ayoréode uyoque, cojñone uyóguiji erámi. A chojninga: “A yipésu cuchisode.” Mu cuchisode unojo cuchisode ga que chiraja darárique. Que chiraja cuchárique.
english: But*Dupade*said: “I haven’t planted a garden yet.” So, he planted things. He planted the*picadéode*trees. He planted the*esodie*trees. He planted things for food all over the world. And he looked at his garden, and saw plants coming up and producing fruit. But he said to himself: “I’ve finished planting the things in my garden, but what is lacking is people to eat it!”

ayoreo: Átaja Pablo Umari ujetiga chucué dáyipie ome chequedíe ujetiga chijnorapise ore, ujetiga chijna bajnoraquei taja ore dirome iji yoquiquigade.
english: *Dupade*, help us right now to be strong, those who are your believers and who trust in you. We want you to set them apart to follow your ways,*Yoqui-Dupade*.

ayoreo: Ore chicáji. Ga ore tujúnapo danochade rĩ, jé aja ore ayode, jé aja potayedie, jé aja ore cóbidie, ore ibotadie. Uje iyataque cho potírique ga qué turuapo dabai te rĩ. Ore chapúsaja.
english: An Ayoré woman might take the fruit herself to her husband and he eats what she offers to him.

ayoreo:
Ga
Eruidaté chojninga: “¿A yicoi jnese jne rĩ a? Acatecãchoi to jne
Dupade ome yu iji udé. Yocate
gajnarei udé. E gusu yuruode jne.
english: Eruidaté was standing there before me. Satan was standing before me, looking for an opportunity to overcome me right here in my house. I started to think, we’re fighting like our forefathers used to fight. I thought, I’m acting as ignorant as they. And I heard myself, but Satan stood his ground in front of me, and was taking over my life, and I couldn’t do what I wanted to do.

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: Qué upo rĩ uje chi uruode uñéngo tocade diríca. Que yiraja puniri uje chi chejna – que yiraja ujeta 3,000 u yugode iji cojñone. Enga yáyipie jĩrique déji diríca Jesus uru casodicaite ome yoque. Enga cojñone ica qué ore chujé ñane. Qué ore déji deréjnane unojo to. Tocade Jesus ojningane cucha.
english: And when we start to notice these things, they will be happening all over the earth. The whole earth will be involved.

ayoreo: Mu todo*Corabé*abai yúode to uje iguiosode jiei.*Corabé*chosõre dabai uje ore chetágome. Uñane ore chojninga: “A yujé iji batigai jne.”
english: But all of the young men disapproved of Slippery Tree Lizard. They said: “Bring him here and we’ll kill him!”

ayoreo: Ga jec u uje yatecã́ri Dupade ome cuchade uje sué yibai. Sué yibai ga que togome yu jeta yimate yu aja uechade cucha dacate gajnaréngone, Satanás ipesudode ome yu; mu yujnusina Dupade uje chi ca cucha uajátique déji ome ga yicatecã́ri ga yojninga: “Dupade, bamo yu uje ayoré gajnareque yu, ga que daijna yu gotique rĩ. Que yipesutique cuchadaté gotique to. Cucha bisidégape yu. Jé basa ga cuchadaté uhua uje bajnué cuchade gai jnese. Jecute yutṍri ua ome udojo cuchade uje que yijnime arínique Que cojñotique chijnime arínique rĩ iji erámi tudé. Enga ajócãre iji yajei.”
english: I prayed fervently to*Dupade*, and he came into my heart. And he helped me. Not only in my state of being an orphan, but also now. I examine my heart, and find that my faith is not little anymore, but much larger.

ayoreo: Chi iñoñai ore sué iñoñai gai gu.
english: It was*Jaboidé*who told us that*Poritai*was the one who made warriors.

ayoreo: “Uacamóño to,” chojninga, “uje ayoré dacasutedie pijositodie u udiji ore.
english: The*Picanerane Clan*hit him, and like the*Posorajane Clan*, they didn’t suck their breath in and out.

ayoreo: *Umaridaté*chojninga: “¡*Jocha*casicaite de a tí!”
english: I said to myself, “I’ll soon reach the other women.” And I did. I found where they had gotten honey, and I found some also and carried it with me. I saw them at a distance. I saw them and right away I saw that they had seen me too. When I arrived I told them I had slept where someone had made a grave, and at that time I had no idea how close they were.

ayoreo: Yosi ti yicatecãraitigade ga jec u uje Espiritu Santo déji yajei. Ga yirasique yu iji quigade uje cho jetiga ca cucha gajnarétique dépoi yajei rĩ uje yiraja udé uje Espíritu Santo e chise yu gu. Jecute chijócãre Satanás iji yajei. Ga cho sere yáyipie to tome cucha dacate gajnaréngone udojo.
english: This is how I talk to*Dupade*about things that are too big for me. Some trials are so big that I can’t get through those ugly things that Satan brings to me. But I remember that*Dupade*said that nothing is too hard for him and then I talk to him and I say, “*Dupade*, you see that I am just a weak, common person and not in anyway a**daijnai*. I’ve never done anything powerful in my life. I’m not worth anything. But, on the other hand, you are greater that everything else. Therefore, I’m calling upon you about these things that*I can’t pull out the meat from*. There’s no-one in this world who can*pull the meat from them*. There is not a*cojñoi*in the world that can do it. So, take this out of my heart.

ayoreo:
Juan a chojninga: “Pedro á, yico ga icoi Dupade iguíjnai jne uje guedé cho jñuque. Yicoi ga yicatecãcoi Dupade iji jne.” Enga Juan, Pedro ore jno ga ore chicáji
Dupade iguíjna cuchábi. Chi ore chajire Dupade iguíjnai piagoi mu ore chimo jnani
iji uje noñaque. Noñai uté qué chirote cojñone ore iji Dupade iguíjnai piagoi.
Noñai u uje chi noñángaja guesi tiji daté. Datéringa uje a chugutana dagutadedie
aja Dupade iguíjnai piagoi. Ga chirote cojñone ore iji. Mu chi Juan, Pedro ore
tṍraji to ome Dupade iguíjnai. Mu ore chajire daquei tiji ga e ore chimo
noñai tiji. Enga qué chirote cojñone tiji Dupade iguíjnai piagoi.
english: One of them said: “Joseph, interpret my dream, also.” That’s what he said to Joseph. “I don’t understand this but, in my dream, it was early morning. And, Joseph, in my dream I was carrying food in a basket on my head. But vultures from above came down and ate the food from the basket I was carrying on my head.” This is what he told Joseph.

ayoreo: Ga adi jé u yocurása casodicaite misionéro uje ore íjnongaja doi. Ore noñome Dupade Uruode iji yocari ujetiga yingongo Dupade Uruode aja ñane. Enga gajnui jec u Jesuse uje chojninga: “Yijíñome baruode iji ore gari, yocacajnaquéode. Enga yoquimeserãsórone ore déji jetiga ore chingo yocacajnaquéode aja ñane jne,” chojninga.*(Juan 17:20,21)*
english: It’s like a woman that makes clay pots; she forms them all very well. Then she bakes the pots. And then she fills them, though they all look alike, the water leaks out of some of them. The ones that have cracks aren’t good for anything. It’s like that with us, for we all have*Dupade*’s Word, but some of us don’t like it, and we are like the leaky pot that won’t hold water so that we can drink from it. However, when someone wants*Dupade*’s Word, he’s like a beautiful water pot that is well-made and holds water. He is like a person that has been taught*Dupade*’s Words by the missionary, and he takes*Dupade*’s Word to other places and teaches our fellow ayoréos about Jesus and tells them: “Jesus told us all about his town in heaven so that all of us can go to be with him.”

ayoreo:
Dupade Uruode
qui u uje chojninga: Gosi a chiraja Dupade Uruode, chiraja Dupade iradedie,
chiraja uruode quigode, mu que chingome dasijnaringuéone ome Dupade jeta
chĩro, enga que toc jeta Dupade jno uté gosi údi to.*(Efesios
2:1, 4, 8, 9)*
english: This is the meaning of*Dupade*’s*Word*: Someone might know*Dupade’s word,*sing his songs, but if he does not confess his sins and ask*Dupade*to forgive them, then*Dupade*will not take such a one to be with him.*(Ephesians 2:1,4,8,9)*

ayoreo:
Ore chisi ome ore ugúchade ome ore: enuéone, duchúbiedie,
paode.
english: The object of the game, besides winning prizes, is to keep in fit condition. The losers give the winners from their possessions, or they hunt honey for the winners. Among the things given would be:  bags, gourds, rope, honey gourds, tapir hide shoes.

ayoreo:
Átaja baruode aja yu gajnerĩ
iji deréjnai tuné, Yoqui*–*Dupade,
guedé jnoringai tuné, Yoqui-Dupade. Ujeta yiraja quitígo.
english: Bring your Words to me this day,*Yoqui*–*Dupade*, this afternoon that I might understand them.

ayoreo: Uje gosi chirajapise enga jorane ore chojninga: “¡Que pinguíraja jnumi gotique!”
english: If a person is especially good at wrestling, this is said of him:  “He’s invincible.”

ayoreo: Yitodódi u Dupade ujuyaquei cuchábi mu que toque go jetiga jé yise.  Ga que yise yiquigade ga que yáta yu aja puágueraque go gajneri. Satanas ipesutique puáguerac. Jiei iji erámi uje ayoredie ore chotigase. Yajéngome ore uruode.
english: *Señora Marina*talked to*Dijaide*about this and told him: “Haven’t you heard that we who believe and follow*Dupade*will be persecuted? And that is what happened to*Dupade*’s Son Jesus also. And he said: “I have done nothing wrong and yet they will kill me and nail me to a cross,” and he said, “how much more will they persecute you who are my disciples.”

ayoreo: Yoque*Dupade*, icatecã́ri ua. Dugúidé urasade uje ore déji dasagode gáyode ujetiga arodipise Dugúidé ga chucué dajéo ome ua, ga chajni babai, ga doi baroquéode to, Yoque*Dupade*. Yiraja uje ca chetaque yoquisatígo cucha ujetiga yicoi trabajándode,  mu bameserãi tu ujetiga yátajago yoquisagode aja ñane ujetiga yátajago Baruode aja yoquisagode cucha.
english: Also help the*Cojñone*who will be hiring us tomorrow. And may their*insides not be hot*as we work for them. May they not experience dislike toward us tomorrow as we*do their commands.*

ayoreo: *Betidaté – Tobité, Bolivia –**08/23/75.* *Marina de Morarie churúsãre uruode nanique enga cháta Dupade uruode aja to.*
english: *Betidaté’s – Tobité, Bolivia – 08/23/75**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie**Scripture portion added.*

ayoreo: Mu yiraja uje ayoréode yocajneque pitoringai enga que yocuñeque déji siñeque jeta ca toi to jne. Gosi a chise deruei te jne, mu eruei tupúsu, ga eruei tupúsu uje que chimopo pucuecaringuérique uñeque rĩ.
english: We know that when a person, whoever he is, dies there’s great sorrowing because their relative has been separated from them. It will be like that when*Dupade*arrives, there will be relatives that will be very sad because they did not believe in*Dupade*. Despite their sadness, it won’t be possible for them to see us in our state of being a relative again. You know that we will never again see those who die.

ayoreo: Yibatigai jogadi Engábi u guidábi nanique. Iapade i tu*Jomainé*. Ité i tu*Podé*. Direquedéjnagose yacoté dai ga i tu*Igoi*. Daté i tu*Jumuné*.
english: The place where I was born was called*Engábi;*that was the name of the little village. My father’s name was*Jomaine.*My mother’s name was*Podé*. My wife’s father was a*Direquedéjnagosi*, and his name was*Igoi*. Her mother’s name was*Jumuné.*

ayoreo: Yacaranguipise ome guidáyode iji erámone uje Dupade Uruode déji guidáyode jnese iji erámone. Yacaranguipise ome guidáyode iji erámone. Dupade uruode déji Tobité, déji Pailón rĩ, déji don Len idai, déji Zapocó rĩ, déji Santa Cruz uéchade rĩ, déji ducuniri uñane, déji Paraguay iji queyéchade, déji Pajeidé idai. Yacaranguipise ome udojo guidáyode uje Dupade Uruode e déji guidáyode ga cojñone, ayoréode chingo yoque Dupade Uruode aja yoque.
english: Dupade – GodDupade’s Words – God’s WordAyoréos – Ayoré peopleCojñone – Non-Ayoré people

ayoreo:
Chi ore ichaga ñane. Chi ca ore
chiraja cuchárique uné diríca iji taningane mu Satanas chãra ore enga jé u uje
ore chiraja paringaquéode, ore chuságaja cuchade uje ore chaguhéta ñane gáyode ome
cuchade.
english: They blamed each other. They were still innocent in the beginning,  but Satan deceived them and then it was that they learned from him all about deceiving and blaming others for things. And that is why God then punished them. And that is why God’s punishment affects us also just as it did the first people that God made, up to this very day. God said to Adam: “You didn’t do what I asked you to do. Because you disobeyed me and ate the fruit from that tree, harmful plants will come up all over this earth. From now on you won’t have free access to food. But you will have to work hard to make things grow to have food to eat. You will work so hard that your forehead will sweat. And only then will you have food to eat. And you will have to fight the harmful weeds that want to choke out your plants.”

ayoreo: Ga yico. Yicagóji dojo. Ga anire, yicoji ta, degúrase ta. Mu cho ta degúrase ji de.
english: We took off. We went over ‘there’. And then, we went another direction toward the camp nearby.

ayoreo: Mu Josédé e areque to iji ujade. E doi*Dupade Uruode*to. E irasique re iji ujade to rĩ.
english: But Peter said: “As if I had any money!”

ayoreo: Ore chojninga: “¿Bayode guejnato isocárigo cho rique? Chi chojninga: “Yayode guejnato a di jne,  ga bajé usereréjna aja iquei, úyujna cuchábe bajé aja iquei. Mu chi bajaque jnacode, chugúpẽre yote dequejnangórone aja iquei jne. A di jne.”
english: He ran till he reached the large village of the **Chamacoco tribe*who are friends of the Ayoré. It’s said he fell down when he got to their village square. And it’s said they all got down on the ground (where he’d fallen).

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Oidábiadé atatagode udojo.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo:
Uacamóño uje páyipiedie eruei
tu. Que yocáyipieraque cuse.
english: But when we don’t believe in God we are saying, ‘I’m greater than God.’ But it’s a very ugly thing to think we’re greater than God, and to claim, “I know more that He does.”

ayoreo: “Que yĩrarare.”
english: *Dajusui*questioned*Adudui*and said, “What did you say?”

ayoreo:
Que eruénique cuse go ome
Dupade, jec u deréjna casé, ga jé upo dirome rĩ. Que chẽcare re. Que
choquíjna Dupade. Mu choquíjnane u yoque. Choquíjnane uyoque uje angoneparique
yógome Dupade gu.
english: David, of long ago who we’ve never seen, said this: ‘I looked in vain for a place in the world where I could hide from God, and I went to the ends of the earth. I went to the depths of the sea, but God was there.’ There is no end to God, he existed in the beginning and will exist in the future. He will never change. God doesn’t grow old, but we grow old. We grow old, because we disobey Him.*(Psalm 139:**1-12**)*

ayoreo: Ayorérique, chequéraque ¿déji jeta ca chosóre datique? Uje chamáñaje ape gu, ujnacare gu. Uaque gosi changureta Jesuse, mu gosi ujetiga cha Dupade úringárique pangurete, mu que toc jeta uaque gosi úringárique cucherãi íjnonguiji Dupade. Úringai ayoré panguresṍri ome Dupade a qué déji. Jé uacamóño uje a ayorérique déji siñec, uaque gosi a ujnacanique a déji tóniñeque, mu uaque gosi úringai cucherãi que íjnoque iji gotique. Chamáñaje jé ape gu.
english: It’s as though*Dupade*stopped communicating with the first people that lived long ago, but he had compassion on us and sent his son Jesus to earth. But his son Jesus left for heaven long ago, and then it was that he sent another to us from heaven. And his name was “*Comforter.*” And, as you know,*Dupade*has shown us his comfort in many ways. We also call the one he sent “*Dupade**’s power the Holy Spirit**”*, and he is our comforter, and comforts and helps us day and night. He helps us when we are sad, and he gives us his power and strengthens us.

ayoreo: Ore asutedie u: Beuidé, Noainé ore.
english: Their chiefs were:*Beuidé,*and*Noainé*.

ayoreo:
Uje õsocacade u ore ome ayoréode uyoque ga
chi, chi misionérone ore jno ome, anire, dacuegode ayoréo. Ga chi ore chise ore
adode ayoréo. Ore chise ayoréode adode, ga que ore áyipieraque déji ujetiga ore
chujé ore jne. Ore áyipie gaidi upúsu Dupade ujetiga Dupade chiqueta ore iji,
anire, ore uniri uté.
english: For a long time we had been hearing that there were people looking for us. None of us Ayoré had any idea at that time that these were people that would be kind to us. That is why we were frightened when we heard that people were looking for us.

ayoreo: José chi chojninga: “Iraja.”
english: Joseph said: “I understand.”

ayoreo:
Uje uacamóño uje Jesuse chi
jnome erámi nanique uje jnoji jnumi nanique, ga jnome gatajnoquei ga chacája
guei abujadie. Ga gusu yóguiji jnumi. Que qué Dupade uñeque dépoji yocajei.
english: *Dupade*said: “My dear son Jesus is back with me, but I will send another to help you,” he said this because he loved us.

ayoreo: *Borade – Asõrenie Ore*
english: *Borade – Asõrenie Ore*

ayoreo: Yocacaranguipísome Dupade uje aníri, Ipeyai ore déji yoque, enga ore chingo yoque ome Dupade. Dupade anguresõre uaque, jé ca yajéjome queyate gajnaréngone ome cucha anínique gotique. Gajnárec jeta yãrángome darai tiji yipajei. Ga adi u Dupade jeta chajéngome ore to jeta di.
english: If we stray from Dupade’s ways, we dishonor*Dupade*, and his beauty is not seen in us. If we stray it’s as though we are dead to*Dupade*’s ways. It’s also as though we are ignorant to the things of Dupade. It’s as though we knew nothing about him. Therefore, I want to tell you about how I used to live.

ayoreo: Ore asuté u: Uniné.
english: Their chief was:*Uniné.*

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie**churúsãre Enueidaté atatagode udojo.*
english: *Enuedaté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo: *Corabé*chojninga: “A gajnáreque yiyangónic u marínga, a jé yabai jne.” Chojningápo rĩ uje ore chise: “A yabai jne yiyangónic.”
english: One of the men said: “I will make an incantation and that will help me find her.”

ayoreo:
Uje jecucha yingome uac uje
dequejnaréngone uje ayorérique daijna dequeserane, ga chubúchu ga que uñeque
chiqueta daguiotique iji Dupade ujuyaquei. Uñeque ujetiga doi ga tãre aja
yúrique ga Dupade*Ujopié*suqué chicai
quedéjnarique ome yoque. Dupade*Ujopié*tu.
english: But if we do not believe in*Dupade*, his arrival, for us, will be dreadful. I dread the coming punishments for us who do not believe. There is no medicine man anywhere powerful enough to blow away*Dupade*’s Son’s arrival.

ayoreo: Jnani bajade ore chujé*Togábi*nanique uje i uñai u*Goródedé*. Ga chi ore chujé. Ga chi daijna ujopierãque  uté  nanique.  Ga chi*Ucatédé*(ore asuté), jõrai*Baráyudé*ga ore u uje ore chujé*Togábi.*
english: *Togábi*was killed by men who lived many years ago*.*(He was also called*Gorodédé.) Togábi*was a medicine man, but one that could curse people. They say it was*Ucatédé*(their chief) and his friend*Baráyudé*that killed*Togábi*.

ayoreo:
Mu chi Eba chi chojnira: “Que
yagu gotique.”
english: But Eve said: “I won’t eat it!”

ayoreo: Jé basa erámi unirisórone ore nína raque uje ore chimate detei taja jnumi. Jé basa ga Dupade anguresórone a ore nina re uje Jesus chimate detesórone aja ore qui.
english: You’ve heard what*Dupade*’s*Word*teaches about going to heaven. “*Dupade*’s road to heaven is a narrow road.” It’s too late to get on that road after Jesus comes back for his believers.”

ayoreo: Mu*Ijnamene*chojninga: “Cachi ayipieraque uje yujo gapua déji ejoi mu jé chujñieta yu gu. Yiya. Yiabi yujo Dosapeque ome yabai,*Oregajnúi*.”
english: Some girls came out to meet me and told me things. They said: “*Ijnamene*, you’ve arrived, but*Jup*i is following you, and wants you to go with him into the camp.”

ayoreo: *Oidábiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: There was a dispute involving*Arangué*and during the battle the*Tiegosode*killed some of the*Totobiegosode*and that’s when they separated from each other. The chief of the*Tiegosode*was*Jochadáquide*. Because*Arangué*and others of her*group*were eating bean seeds that had been saved for planting,*Jochadáquide*sent people to kill them.

ayoreo:
Ayoréode uje
déji erámi jeta ore nóna ñane uje ore bac, enga uñai changureta Dupade, jé uñai
que changureta to go. Uñai jno ome gatajnoquei, jé basa ga uñai jnusi dajõrai.
Que nóna dajõrai taja gatajnoquei gotique gu uje que changureta Dupade to. Uñai
changureta, enga ore jnome gatei. Mu jnumi tu enga ayoréode nóna ñane aja
dabatigade, jé ore nóna ñane aja dasoránenie, jé yisape degúi jnese. Mu a chise
Dupade diringai jeta chise yoquibatigai uje yoquiquigode u iji erámi enga jno
yoquijõrai udé uje changureta Dupade. 
Enga unojo u uje Dupade ojningai u jetiga yanguretágo Dupade jnese iji
ne jnumi.
english: So that’s why I’m asking you to tell us if you are ready to put your faith in*Dupade*. And if you don’t let us know now, don’t wait for another year. For*Dupade*’s*Word*says that if you hear*His Word*today, don’t say, “I might believe in Jesus next year or some time in the future.” Today is the day to believe in*Dupade*and if you do, you will have joy in hearing his*Word*because now you believe it. There’s coming a day that, if you do not put your faith in*Dupade*, you will miss us, or miss your mother or your father, because you can’t go where we believers go.

ayoreo:
Mu quigade ga
uté Ajei chi chacate darásade ore ome Dupade Uruode ujade. Que chirajapise
Dupade Uruode mu chacate ore garósi, garósi, garósi. Ga jé isocai u.
english: You know that don Len and Ecarai went to visit others and I prayed for them after they left for six Sundays. And then, they came back and Davidé began telling me about*Dupade*’s*Word*. But Pajei and the others wouldn’t listen to*Dupade*’s*Words,*and that’s how it was with that rich man in the Bible. But afterwards don Len and Ecarai tried to teach Pajei and them. But they told Len and Ecarai: “There’s nothing to it.” They told them the only*Dupade*they wanted to worship was women. They just wanted to go after them and love women.

ayoreo:
Yipota jetiga yiraja darujna
aone. Ga yajíraja quigode. Jetiga yacate yujodie chequei to. Mu etoque ome yu
jetiga yiraja. Que yiraja gotique uje que yiraja go. Ipota jetiga Ucaré ore
chatatarása dadode to, Jómone ore. Ca ore chajéngome. Que cuchárique déji go.
Ore hermánopíseráse uyoque to jne rĩ iji de diri uje Josuedé, Bai ore
chingo yoque. Ga e chĩro yoquisijnaringuéone.
english: Jesus counseled his disciples and he said to them: “People are going to make trouble for you. Satan is going to make trouble for you. He will deceive some of you. People will say you are crazy. The things they say about you will be ugly. But that’s because they don’t believe in Dupade, but you do.”

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre Guié iradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Guié – Tobité, Bolivia – 1965.*

ayoreo: *Enueidaté bijna nanique enga chise yodi iji poridie:*
english: *Jocha – Enuedaté’s name before she had children *Enuedaté – Tobité, Bolivia – 1960**Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie*
*Amomégosode***–**“People of*amomé*.”

ayoreo: Chiese yãcorenie chiraja ayoréode uyoque ga u oidi jnani bajaque ayoré ga chi chojninga: “Boyo iji ayoréode uyoque uje jocãratade udore gu. Ga boyo.”
english: Later on he killed ‘big-bellied pigs.’ And when he was a young man he would kill whole troops of wild pigs. And that’s when he became known as the great hunter of wild pigs. And it’s also when the wild pigs got fed up with him because of how many pigs he was killing.

ayoreo: Chi Cochocoigosode ore chicáji Jnupedogosode nanique iji diri uté nanique uje chuque misionérone iji. Jiei bajade uje e ore jno, mu uñane qué ore sase. Ijmai déji bajade ore ujai nanique enga jno to. Mu Caratábide chicáji dejnai bajaque ga déji uté uje chudute cuchárique. A misionérone u enga ore catecã́ri ñane uje ore chicáji uté yui. Todo ga chayo ga chicáji degúi ujetiga chingo darásade iji uté. Chojninga: “¡Cojñotígo e di é, ayóyome ore!” Enga urasade ore chayo jnese.
english: The men from the*Cochocóigosode*grouop were going off to the*Jnupedógosode*the day the men were killed. Many of them had already left and others were getting ready to follow.*Ijmai*was in the group who had already left.

ayoreo:
Gusu ua uje
bajnué cuchade jnese. Bajnué pitoringai gai. Bajnué Satanás, irógode gai. Uje
Davidé chacate yoque gajnerĩ ga ãro yocate gajnaréngone gajnerĩ,
pisijnaringuéone iji pajéode.
english: We know that good things come from you whenever we meet to study your words*.*Watch over us tonight and protect all of us from Satan. Forbid*our-nearnesses*to him. You are the only one we obey every day. We can not see you, but you can see us and are with us right now.

ayoreo:
José chojninga: “Iraja. Yiraja
arĩa barigode. Udore imoi chi u ujetiga chiroque ua iji quigade. Bajne tu úva
adie ujetiga apésu eduguéjna cuchábi jotai vínoi ome udire. Enga uacarigode
quigode u unojo. Mu que yirajatíga go. Dupade irajatíga uje chingome uaque,”
José chojninga.
english: Before this, two others had told Joseph their dreams.

ayoreo:
Jecute
jecucha yingome uaque jeta gosi pota nóna darasade, jeoga gotoa pota nóna
dabai, jeoga gosi pota nóna dacoté ore to, ga ango Dupade. Ga Dupade chĩro
basijnaringuéone, uje Ujnacari Jesus cho basijnaringuéone oré uje toji curesei
gai. Ga Dupade chinoningase ua enga báringai ayoré pijõre jogadi tu ome Dupade
uje Dupade chimesẽre ua. Dupade a churúsãre bei iji gatajnoquei.
english: But it is a good thing for us who follow*Dupade*’s*Words*to change our ways, then*the**Cojñone*believers won’t know what to say about us. It is also good for us to have*pretty**testimonies*. When we change our ways we no longer do the things we used to do. We no long tie our hair like our forefathers did. We no longer go without clothing. We no longer kill the*Cojñone**.*We don’t kill our fellow*A**yoréos*. All that is left for us to do are other things like these and to trust in*Dupade*so that when he comes for us we can all go to Him.

ayoreo: *Joyce Davis Buchegger churúsãre piradedie udaji nanique.*
english: *Transcribed by: Joyce Davis Buchegger*

ayoreo:
“Jec ũré enga uacarajáyo Yapade,” chiese
Jesus chojninga. Chujé yógaja jnumone ome darúo.
english: And, in my dream that night, I prayed, and I said: “*Dupade*! I’m talking to you right now. I’m very thankful that your Son Jesus appeared to me and talked to me about you, and that is why I am talking to you about myself, because I’m in lots of pain and I ask you to help me.” This is what I seemed to say to*Dupade*in my dream.  “Do something about my sickness, please,” I said in my dream, praying to*Dupade*. “I’m so thankful that your Son Jesus appeared to me,” I said to him.

ayoreo:
Ãro
yocurõsoringuéone iji yocúdode jnese, Dupade. Yocurajédie, yoquejnarec uñane,
Dupade, uje déji chequedíe, disiode ore, jnanione ore jnese, Dupade. Ãro
urõsoringuéone iji yocúdode jnese, Dupade. Etoguipise yoque, Dupade, jeta yico
dínique jnese, Dupade, ome yocúringai ayoré ijõre.
english: Get rid of the pain we have in our bodies,*Dupade*. Heal our coughs and the other illnesses we have,*Dupade*– do this for the women, the children, and the men, all of them. Erase the pain of all our bodies,*Dupade*. May we be strong,*Dupade,*so that we can face each day in good health.

ayoreo:
Yicatecã́ri ua
ome yocurása di ejode, Rósadé, Soríané ore. Udojo u uje ore changureta ua.
Yiraja uje yiquérango iji guidai to mu baruode batigai tu jeta yocuñeque jno
maringa, jetiga ca gosi cha baruode iji erámi. Adi u Rósadé ore jeta ore
chajnerame baruode iji yocacajnaquéode iji dajesúrigo erámio. Dupade,
yicatecã́ri ua ome cuchade jnese ome bajnacari Jesute i, amẽ.
english: I ask you right now, Lord Jesus, to really help your believers here*where your words land*. Your Father, Lord Jesus, finished his work long ago on this day, and the only thing he wanted us to do on this day was to rest in camp. For this reason, we ask you to kindly bless us with your words, that we*only*think about your words today. But,*Yoque Dupade*, we know we do have lots of work to do. And even though we do our work as unto you, don’t let anything bad happen to us,*Yoque Dupade.*I pray for your believers here in Tobité to grow in their faith, so that we might rejoice together and be happy.

ayoreo:
Mu que yimo
ujeta ojnigai u yu to. Que yimo ujeta ojningane u Pablo, Tito, Timotéo ore uje
chojninga: “Ijíñome uaque iji Tobité. Yiroque uágaja Tobité ujetiga acacho
ayoréode ore iji,” a chojninga. Mu que gusu unojo ore. Que gusu Davidé uyu, que
gusu Pablo ore nanique ujetiga noñome yóguiji ne mu jé noñome yocujade iji to.
A noñome Josuédé, Jómoné ore uje ore chiraja*Dupade**Uruode*to. Jé basa
ga ojningane u unojo ujetiga jnani bajade uje jé yocajnúone u Pablo uje
chojningame Tito: “Tito á! yijíñome ua iji yote quedéjnai ajã́ro,” chojninga.
“Ore idáyabi uje déji yote quedéjnai ajã́ro enga bo*Dupade**Uruode*údi iji ore
ujade,” chojninga. “Enga ango*Dupade**Uruode*amirone aja ore uje cáma
yingogome ore to diríca,” chojninga.
english: Certain things were important to the little bird: when they were away from camp they were not to eat or drink water. And only after they were whipped after returning, could they drink water.

ayoreo: Uje quene yicagóji Tobité nanique iji taningai, enga ité, yinanie, uyoque yujégo yogaja buéyode gáyode. Uje yirõco Tobité yui, mu yai yusṍrone ore chachai yoque. Uje ore chudute uje chi ore yui acoté chicáji Tobité enga ore pota jeta ore chachai uaté. Uje yĩríngo enga ore chojningame ité: “Yoco yocáyugu uje yipéco bajnusiétigade nanique. Ujetiga yirajágorase uje ueradei babai ore áyipiedie nanique enga, a ca yujégorase ore.”
english: The first time we went into Tobité‚ my mother, my sisters, and I had to ride oxen.  As we were arriving, we were met by my father’s killers. When they heard that the wife of one of their victims was coming into camp, they wanted to go out to meet her. When they met her, they said to my mother: “We are very sorry for the sorrow we have caused you. If we had known then what we know now, we would never have done it.”

ayoreo: Gosi chajire udojo, purupatáringa, chajire Dupade ojningane, chojninga:‘Que purupatáringai déji yu gotique iji uné darujna aro.” Déji*Dupade Uruode*uje purupatáringane a déji ajei te, mu gosi que pésu ga gosi chojninga: “Yacaranguipis uje cáma chise yu go ga yujnué purupatáringane gáyode gu yajirigode Dupade urúo.”
english: “I know that I tend to lie, but when I keep reading*Dupade’s Word*, it keeps me from lying,” a person might say to himself. When that person keeps reading*Dupade’s Word*he also might say, “I don’t flirt and play around now.”

ayoreo: *Jnaniné*uyu yojninga*:*“Ẽ́*Chequé Dago*, ãrame yu. Yijina yurasade ore to. Deroco yui jeta tṍra yugúchade, yacoté. Babe yu gu,” yojningame*Chequé Dago*. “Be bajogadi gotique ga yimóji yijogadi. Jnani yu. Yabode déji degúi.”
english: *Cheque Dago*came to our village, and*Cheque Dago*took shelter where I was in the shade. (But*Jnaniné*wouldn’t let her in.)

ayoreo: Yugúsiji uta ica yidai Urucu. Mu chajẽ táguedie di. Ga ore chojninga: “Ebedérodaté yá, ¿adute to a? Ujetiga chi ajócãre Goránaté ore iji udé jne.”
english: Margarita came, and she taught us. Margarita said: “Carolínadaté, what do you want to hear?”

ayoreo:
“Yacaranguipis ome*Dupade
Uruode*ojningane unojo. Cáma pisijnaringuei chise yu gotique uje*Dupade Uruode*chãraque yu gu,”
chojninga.*(Salmo 119:11)*
english: This explains the function of a mirror. Therefore, I tell you to notice that if someone wants a clean face, his mirror will not miss a thing. He will wash his face and look into the mirror again and say, “Now it’s clean. I’m so thankful.”

ayoreo:
Enga chi ore chojninga: “¡Yico á!
Yijiínango to Jacób ore Angarádejnai Dupade uje e di! Yijínango to
Yocangarádejna Dupade. Yocangaráni tu uje e di tiji yoque.” Chi ayoréode yocuruode
a u unojo to ujetiga Dupade di jne iji ne jnumi.
english: I’d be so happy if our ways and our words were like those of Isaiah and the other men of old. They didn’t say it exactly this way, but I think that this is what we and the Cojñone will say to each other: “Let’s go, let’s go with*Dupade*, to the one that the ancient Jacob listened to long ago. He is the one, according to the visions*Dupade*gave to Isaiah, that is coming back again.”

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*Tie Quedéjnai*, ga jiei ore.
english: *Direquedéjnagosipísode (Unirequedéjnagosode) –*“Original people of the strange day (land).”

ayoreo: Uje pésu ore ájningane iji ore ajei uje chi ore chujé jnani bajade ore iñoñai, ome*Jaboidé*. Jé u uje dacasutedie cucherane chi ore ipesungóri tu ore iñoñai (*Poritai*). Chi dacasutedie ore ipesungóri udé, ome*Jaboidé*.
english: There are other things we’ve been told that pertain to Ayoré warriors.

ayoreo: Uñane chojninga: “¡Pu a! asa gajnerí. ¡Pu a! asa gajnerí.” Jiei ore iji carujnangue údi. Ore chajo údi ga guesa údi.
english: Others would encourage their relatives and try to put charms on them so they could reach the top. They would say: “You can do it*¡pu jõ!*You can do it*¡pu jõ!”*

ayoreo: Chi daijnane ore chujé dajnu ditáji ga pujopie tṍraji ore ome ore enga jé daijna ore.
english: The medicine men bite to pull venom out of the one who has been bitten by a rattlesnake. The medicine men are powerful. To heal people who have been bitten they pull the venom out of them. According to the medicine men who lived long ago, power comes to the medicine men from the twisted wild pineapple plant’s fibers. They open themselves up and power presents itself to them from the twisted wild pineapple plant. By using the twisted wild pineapple plant, those who are already medicine men are able to bestow power to heal on other men.

ayoreo: Adi u Eri uje chise yóguiji de diríca ga jnusina yogome Dupade Uruode, enga e que toc jeta yipéco yocuru gajnarécho ome*queyujórique*ayoré. Jeta jé adi tu ome cuchade. Jé ca yajénique dotãre ome yujórique ayoré. Jé ca yaguhéta yujórique ayoré gai ome cucha gajnarétique. Gajnáreque udé, chamáñaje Dupade urúo gu. Dupade ajéode u ujeta yacaco yógome iji deréjnane jnese. Jé ca*queyurúrigo*gajnaréngome yujórique ayoré. Jeta yuruode gajnaréngome yujórique ayoré ga e yique. Jeta Dupade di jne mu yipógu ica yuruode, mu yingome uje yuruode gajnarécho ome yujórique ayoré ica ga yate gajnareque. Ga jé yique.
english: Up there, in*Dupade*’s presence, all is beautiful. As believers we should be hopeful, for that beautiful land is to be our land. If you women are Dupade’s believers, that beautiful land in heaven is your land. **’s ways****:**

ayoreo:
Mu jé ũrepise Dupade uje, jé
ũrepise Satanas uje, a ca yajo cuchade adie unaji ga que yimóngo pajei
gátigárique go. A jé yoquirasique ñane. Que pajei dotáningánique déji yoc. Que
yajéngome yocúringárique choquíbi. Mu yajo unojo ga jé Satanas píode déji to ga
jé u uje yirajágo cucha sijnángone.
english: And Satan said: “Why are you so afraid all the time? Won’t that fruit taste good?” Now, you know, when fruit is very ripe it becomes very appealing!” The fruit was just hanging there, tempting our mother Eve.

ayoreo: Enga chi íjnoque tiempo dirome. Íjnoque tiempo ujetiga qué ayorérique chojninga: “Jesuse, yanguretape ua to gajiné iji deréjnai tudé. Uje e bãri enga yimo ua ome yedodie gu.”
english: This is what Jesus counseled people to do. His counsel wasn’t only for the people then, but Jesus counsels us today, and his counsel is the same for all of us who are his disciples whatever our generation.

ayoreo: *Icadiguédé*, ore iroguebeque, ore chajmára iradedie.
english: The songs  of **Icadiguédé*are some that we really look forward to hearing! People are always asking him to sing.

ayoreo:
¡Jõ, a yicorási iji ta ga yinínango
yui! Ga ore chacãra uje a yugúcorási ta ga ore chojninga: “¡Yico, yicagoi
cucarani iji te ga!”*(Isaías 2:3)*Ga ore nína yui uje ore chiraja uje íjnoque dequejnaréngone iji gu.
english: And that is like what Isaiah tells us about and says: All the nations of the world will join in saying: ‘Come on! Let’s go, let’s go to that beautiful land.’ It will be a beautiful land. But they’re not talking about the woods that I know about where there was honey, and turtles, and other jungle foods. But Isaiah tells us that it could be likened to a mountain. And he says that*Dupade*’s land it like that mountain over there. Why, if we would leave right now, we’d be so excited. When we got there they would say: “Come on! Let’s go to that mountain! (Isaiah 2:3) We would rejoice because there is no sickness there.”

ayoreo:
Yocacaranguipis ome unojo Dupade
Uru garósiode. Jé gusu yatatagode, Dupade Urúo, ome yoque iji guedé jnoringai.
Mu que yiraja jeta uacuñeque déji duruñeque jeta gosi pota enga chope quique
Dupade Uru garósirigo to ome yoque.
english: Dupade’s words say: “Don’t think you are a great teacher. This is a wrong attitude, according to Dupade. Don’t lord it over people.” You know that when someone is a real leader, he doesn’t brag about being a great teacher. He knows what Dupade’s word’s say about wanting to be the big boss, and he understands that it is something we should not do. And as believers, we should obey Dupade. We should not want to be greater than others. We should be a teacher not a boss of those we teach.

ayoreo: Yiquejnángome jocaratade ga*Ajópedái*chise yóguiji ta. Mu jé gaidirase ore iji degúi. Mu*Oguéa*,*Usigaidatéa*ore chaquĩ́ra. Ore chojninga: “Ácari te enga bo.”
english: Two returned to the camp. And we went far away. We kept running from the warriors and ran far away.

ayoreo:
Mu uje ore
chiraja uje Jesuse jé ũré uje ecápo rĩ ga que ore chajirape cuchárique. Chi
jĩrique ore jno ga ore chajnésarapo Jerusalen rĩ ujetiga ore chingo cucha
dacadode udore ome Jesus iroguipísode. Mu chi udojo ore chojninga: “Yimóngosi
Jesus ucacabí casé diríca ga e ecasiapo rĩ, ga a jec ũrepísome darúrigo ome
darijíningai iji datoringai.”
english: But they said: “Oh, stay awhile, eat our food.”

ayoreo:
Gosi atígo gajnárecho ome Dupade
jeta cho jmiti, jmiti ome dojode*Dupade*anguretasóño
ga déji iji de. Jetiga gosi pésu cucha gajnarétique jeta gajnáreque ipesudode
cucha enga déji jeta ore tṍra guesi iji*Dupade
Uruode*unojo. Dupade ujnacari Jesucrísto adode déji*Dupade Uruode*, enga cho yocúringane piquetaque isocade ome yoque. *Dupade Uruode*chingo yu iji darujna aode uje chi*Dupade Uruode*gajnui tu aníri ajicaradéjnai, mu ise ga jec ũré*Dupade Uruode*uje ayorérique a ca déji siñeque jetiga ca ajicaratique déji iguíjnai. Chajire dedodie iji aníri ajicarani ga chimo uje edodie uécha uñai topié. Jé basa ga iguirode catája. Jé basa icha uécha uñai ga gajnúi jec u*Dupade Uruode*uje*Dupade Uruode*unojo chingo yoque jnese cucha utatade iji yoc. Chi uje ayorei ca chajire re iji ajicarani enga jé ca chimo dedodie jeta ichai. Adi jec u*Dupade Uruode*, a yajico*Dupade Uruode*enga*Dupade Uruode*chingo yoque. Ujeta yingongo yoquipesudode cucha gajnárecho – yoquipesudode, yocuru patáringane, yocoriquéode aja Dupade enga Dupade chĩro, enga gajnui u uje gosi churu dedodie iji aníri yodi ga chajirapo dajicarani rĩ enga jé chimo uje que iguínique cuse gotique.
english: Is there anyone, anywhere, who can say, ‘*Dupade’s Word*just came into being’? Are there people anywhere, who after seeing that they are sinners, can do something to get rid of their sin? Of course, not, only*Dupade’s Word*tells us how to do that – for it contains the answers to everything.

ayoreo: Bajnui a u San Pablo gajnerĩ. Uje changureta Jesuse nanique ga chi jĩraque chacate ore ome Dupade adode.  Chi jé qué todoba cojñone. Chagutu rome Dupade adode, mu Dupade jé chirógaja. Jé jno irajatigade údi. Adi jé u ua jetiga angureta Dupade ga bagutu ua ome Jesuse adode ujetiga acate bojode ayoréo. Mu quigade que agutúpo ua ome rĩ. Jesuse tu bátajasṍri gu. Jesuse u uje chacate ore mu oidi tu babijnani ome dacajnaquéode. Apota jeta Jesuse chijna babijnani ga ango ua aja Jesuse. Ujeta ojninga: “Jesuse, ajna yibijnani ga yacate yujode ayoréo.” Ango ua aja Jesus ujetiga acatecã́ri bayé Dupade.
english: Paul’s name was Saul in the beginning, but when Saul believed in Jesus,*Dupade*changed his name to Saint Paul.

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*D**ajeguéo*.
english: Their land was ‘*dajegueo*’. Their chiefs were:*Guebei,*and*Cajodáquide*.

ayoreo: Jecucha yingome uaque uje chi ayoré, jeoga chequé, jeoga cojñoi, jeoga uñeque, yocajnui tu cojñoc ugucharãi uje chi chojninga uje chimo yocai jnani bajai Abraham: “Ango yesade ore iji jnumi enga ca ore chicai gajño pioi to. Que yisapo yijóningai iji rĩ,” chojninga. “A ayoréode, Dupade guetesórone ore yocuruode taratágome jeta yisi ore ome Dupade,” chojninga. “Aroque Dupade anguresõre uñéngaja jnumi enga chacate yijorane ore,” chojninga.
english: We know that*Dupade*’s*Son*is coming back again. Therefore, if you want your friends to go to heaven, then tell them the news about Jesus coming back for us who believe in Him.

ayoreo: Jnusina dajai uje Ayoré. Uásejna ore tagu yacorenie ga chosõripise dajai mu que iguiotique cuse ga ore gajnepíode garósi iji yacore. Chajire dajai. Chojninga: “Yajire bora póroro mu que gajnepítique cuse ga yosõripis.”
english: She remembers*Jonoiné*for*Jonoiné*really killed jaguars, non-Ayoreos, and Ayoreos, and he told the truth. He was like the “south winds!” He had many, many victims.

ayoreo: Chi guei ca tagu uté ore jutícabia to. Ga jno ga tibágui nero, mu chi chise nero abai ujuya gajnei gase ga chi chojninga: “Ujuya yoc iji yoquidai. Uje guérique que tagu yoc!”
english: *Dachapiboraté*’s wife’s brother said: “I’m going to go to see my sister and her family.”

ayoreo: Chi di ji degúi, ga chojningame dacoté: “A yijína cuchátique.” Chiraja uje jotábia dési ijoyade gai te.
english: To get enough water Falling Rain was wringing the hair that grows right by his ear into his gourd cup and it filled with water, but there was a river plant floating on top.

ayoreo: Ujñarone u ore uruode ga chubúchu ga tṍraji. Daijnane ore chigase ga chijnímiiji posode ayoré. Daijna cuchábode ore. Ore iquetaque posode ayoré; ore chijnímiji ore gu. Pujopie garani dajnu ditáji ome jnacare bajade uje daijna ore. Ga ore tocade ga pujopie chiqueta raja ore iji dajnu ditáji ga daijna ore ga chijnímiji ore.
english: Ayoré persons who understand witchcraft know what the antidotes are and they can heal people who have been bitten by snakes. Breath, that is their word for blowing, and medicine comes out of them when they blow.

ayoreo: Chiese ore apose gatéode ga ca guérique tagu ayorérique.
english: And then they heard the eerie sound approaching and they said: “¡Set a barricade on fire!”

ayoreo:
Jec ũré uje
chi*Dupade*chojningame Moises
nanique: “Boyo iji ore ujade ga yejna ore,” chojninga, “uje ore chujñieta
yuruode gu.” Uje ore chujñieta ga ore cho*Dupade*iji deduguéjnai uñai ipesudi aquirájna gajnúrique.*Dupade*anguresṍri jé chaquĩ́ra jóroque*Dupade*, mu*Dupade*chajo
raja, uje chamáñaje, aníre gu, chetaque ore adode ga que doi ga tangarápoi
danguresṍri rĩ uje chaquĩ́ra. Que tangarápoi danguresṍri uruode gotique
rĩ.
english: *(1 Corinthians 10:11)*Korah and his friends didn’t like it that Moses and Aaron were their leaders. So one day they scolded them, and*Dupade*heard it and he defended his faithful servants, Moses and Aaron. He made the earth to open up under Korah and them and they fell in. The earth closed over them, and*Dupade*sent a consuming fire to burn up the other 250 men who followed Korah, and immediately fire consumed them.*Járipe!*

ayoreo:
San Pablo chojninga: ‘Yocacajnaquéode
unojo cucha bisidécho, pũrusoringuéone ome anírone, Dupade guetesórone ore iji
yoque.’*(1 Corintios 1:18-25)* Enga ise ga jec ũré, a que
yipótago Dupade ga iyieja conferencia yui jeta yicoi Dupade Uruode gajnerĩ.
Iyieja yui ga iramurase Dupade Uruode iguíjnai tiji yajei. Enga gajnui jec u
uje jecucha cucha bisidégode u ga que yirajágo quitique ga suru
yocangoninguinone bisidec. Mu gosode uje changureta Dupade ga unere Dupade Uruode yui tome ore ga udore ore uje angoninguinone guesája, ore áyipie angoninguinone guesája to. Ga unojo u uje a yocajnúone u unojo uje Isaías ojningane u uje chojninga: ‘Punirone iji erámi jnese, ore doi ga ore chojninga, ‘¡Yico á! Yicoi Dupade Idai te gá…’
english: It is like when we Ayoreos were still in the woods long ago and would call to each other.*Dupade*’s words are calling to us, inviting us to go to his city, saying: “Come on! Let’s go to*Dupade*’s house!” That’s what*Dupade*’s words tell us when it speaks of*Dupade*’s kingdom. When it says*Dupade*’s kingdom, it’s referring to his land. I don’t understand the word ‘kingdom,’ because it is the Cojñone’s word. We know, and you might know what the word means. A kingdom refers to a King’s land, the land where he rules, that is it’s meaning. Therefore, heaven is like*Dupade*’s land up there, a mountain peak where there is no sickness. We like that it is our destination, and there’s nothing about it that we don’t like. And what I’m telling us is that we should be saying that we truly believe in*Dupade*.

ayoreo: Chi chacho ore uje moi tome,*Corabé*. Chi jno. Cheru carujnangue. Ore chucué jeta ore doi. Ore chojninga: “Jé yacoté jeta yise jne. Jé yacoté jeta ca yiya jne.” Ore potapísa uje póroro gu. Ore chichaga dajéode taja jnese, jnacarode. Ore chojninga: “A yacotérase, a yacotérase.”
english: The young men hoped that they could get together with*Corabé*, but she wouldn’t go to them. She just flirted with them. They really wanted her for a fiancée. But*Corabé’s*little husband was going to be*Carujnangue Erusṍrami*(Lizard).  (*Carujnangue is the name of a tree with a slippery trunk, and Erusṍrami*is the term for ‘the small one who climbs’.

ayoreo: Mu qué adi jé u cojñoque bajade uje cojñone chi ore chotigase. Adi u uje yotigágo Meráminé, Juan ore. Uje yojníjo: “Arapataque Meráminé ore.”
english: But some of the first*cojñone*that we had contact with were unbelievers. And that is why we doubted the things that Meráminé, and Juan told us. And we said: “Meráminé and them are liars.”

ayoreo:
Yingome Pablo atuatigade ome
yoque. Atuatigade jiei iji úringai sijnac. Atuatigade uje cho damajo Jesuse anguresórone. Mu chise diri uje Pablo chojningame Jesuse:
“¡Yangaráni á! ¿Bapotigátique a ujetiga yipésu jne?”
english: I’ve told you about how Paul was. He did many bad things. He killed and persecuted those who believed in Jesus. But one day Paul said to Jesus: “You are my Lord! What is it you want me to do?”

ayoreo:
Señora Marina, bajneque
yuruode, uje yipota ujetiga araja yipesudode cucha gajnarécho iji aníri, yuniri
udé. Iyajmánguipíse uje ujnusina anírone, yipesudode cucha gajnárecho. Ga
yipota ujetiga yibo, mu ujnusina cuchade uje gajnárecho iji yuniri, ga yipota
ujetiga yibo. Mu Dupade Uruode u ujetiga yibópise uje yacate ore ome Dupade
uruode nanique enga yátaja ua to uje bajnerame udore ome yocuruode.
english: *Señora Marina (Maxine Morarie)*, these words of mine are for you, because I want you to know the ugly things I have done here where I now live. For a long time I’ve been thinking about all the things I’ve done. I am very sad when I think about how bad things are here.

ayoreo: Ga yuruode qui u uje ayoréode yocanguretaretigai, ore anguretigai ca chojmára. Jé chojmára. Udore misionéronie que ore cãra danguretique cucha uñai gotique. Ga gosi a changureta Dupade ga gosi anguretaretigai jé u misionéro ore anguretaretigai. Mu ore irajatigai sué yoquibai. Mu que ore ijnoraquei, Dupade ijnoraquei, que sue uaque gosi gai gotique. Unojo yuruode unojo u uje qué yingo Dupade uruode aja don Juan, don Juan casicaite. Don Juan casicaite chojninga: “Ayorérique a déji jetiga ayorérique sue yoquibai,” chojninga. “Que chiraja darujna aórique, mu anguretaretigai u uje Dupade chisi ome dajopié tome, ga sue yoquibai ome danguretaretigai,” chojninga.
english: Paul said to Tito: “I see that you are not a Jew, but you surpass us Jews in the way you serve*Dupade*.”

ayoreo:
Chi chojninga que, eduguéjna
cuchábi: “Que yirasique yu ome yurigode go. Daijna gajnaréngone que ore
chajnerame yurírique go ome yurigode jeta ore chiraja,” Faraón chi chojninga, “Mu
a yejna daijna gajnaréngone ore iji erámone,” chi Faraón chojninga. “Uje que
pirajaque yurigode go ome ore.”
english: Joseph hurried to pray to*Dupade*that he would tell him the meaning of Pharaoh’s dreams.

ayoreo: Mu disi ejode uyoque ga ujetiga yanguretágo Dupade ga yocajnei tu*nuevo testamento*, enga a ca yicórase pacajnaqueque diyajmane unojo gotique. Mu yacáco yógaja eduguéjnai David uté. David a chimo jóronga uje urasade ore charásu ga sose Dupade yui. Ga Dupade chĩro isijnaringuéone enga chinguíra darádie aja isijnaringuéone jogadode enga íjnongapo rĩ.
english: “If a young woman sleeps with a young man she isn’t married to, kill that young woman, or if a young man has sex with a young woman without marrying her, kill him.”That is what Moses said. He also said: “Do this so that this sin will not be named among you.”Those things were written in the Old Testament, the teachings that our forefathers obeyed. That was before Jesus come to earth to die for our sins.

ayoreo: E ore jno ga ore basiji ta, mu e yicopo rĩ. E ore chise degúi iji te bajade. Enga*Garáijnainái*chachai yu rĩ, ga yisíome yi ome ga chijnoc. Degu te. Jé u gajnerĩ uje yijíñongome yoquigode gajnerĩ iji diri.
english: And then we returned to our campsite.  And everyone was out getting wild pineapple food called*doridie.*Some of us saw the*orojno*bee. They said: “Wait for us to get the honey, and then we’ll leave when the sun is over there and the next place we come to will be our camp. And we’ll be carrying our salt.”

ayoreo: Que Picanerane que ore chejna dajénique aja guetique iji darurui gai.
english: But not the Picanerane. They didn’t have to work themselves up to kill victims.

ayoreo:
Mu ore
chojninga: “A jé ca adute to uje ore chujé yoqueduguéjna case Jesus, ga que jé
imóngopo go, ga a ca yimóngopo to jne rĩ.” Ga Jesus
chojninga: “¡Yiraja! Jé go uje a ore angurete gotique diríca mu ome
dejnaréngone, mu jé ore chujé.” Chi Jesus uruode a udojo mu que ore chiraja
Jesuse go uje catecã́ri ore.
english: Jesus said: “That’s how it is with what will happen to me. The people are going to kill me, and it’s going to make them very happy to see me dead. On the other hand, my believers will miss me, and they will be sorrowful, but then they’ll see me again. That’s because I’m going to come back to life.” This is how Jesus explained to them what he meant when he said they would be sorrowful and then they would be happy.

ayoreo: Jnacari chingome yu uje pota*Maria*. Chojninga: “*Manuelné*, ango*Maria*ga yacoté jne.”
english: There was a young man who wanted to make love to*Maria*. He said to*Manuelné*: “*Manuelné,*tell*Maria*to be my wife.”

ayoreo:
Ga Jesus
chacãra dacajnaquéode aja cuchade. Jecute jé ojningane chojninga ujeta ca darasade
chiraja quitígo ome. Que yiraja jeta jé u, mu a jé u uje chojninga: “Asi ome
gajnúone ome ore,” chojninga. “A ore chiraja quigode jnenga jĩrique ore
chiraja yocojningane cucha,” chojninga. “Mu ujetiga ore cho cucha bisidéguiji
yacajnaquéode enga a yirajágo jne uje ore cho cucha bisidéguiji yu to, enga jé
u uje yipógu yacajnaquéode quigode ome udore ore.”
english: And so it was that Jesus continued to teach with parables. It was the way Jesus told things so that some*would**not understand*. I don’t know if it was that way, but I believe it was, for Jesus said: “Give them parables, and if they figure them out, they will quickly understand what we are teaching. If they disregard my teachings as nonsense, then we will know that they also think I am not anyone to pay attention to, and from people like that, I will hide the meaning of my messages.”

ayoreo: Yirasique yu uje ore chujé tãrara ga cho ujetiga cho disiode uje yoquirasique yocabode yui uje yajnángoji data. Yirasique yui uje ore chujé tãrara. Yisárase cuchade mu yojninga, ‘E yiya jne.’ Yiya ga yajnai to jne Dupade iguíjnai udé. Chãje yirasique yui gu. Gajnui u disiode uje yajnángoji datá ga yoquirasique udé Dupade iguíjnai.
english: I’m happy when they ring the bell for church, and it’s like the kind of happiness we felt when we were children and went about with our friends. I look forward to them ringing the bell. I am about to start doing something, but when I hear the bell, I say, I’m going to do this later. I stop what I’m doing and take off for God’s house. It’s because I’ve been happily looking forward to it. I’m like a child getting together with my friends, that’s how happy we are to be going to church.

ayoreo: *Duábidácabi*chi chĩ́cara ore iji dajei. Chi chojninga: “Yĩ́cara ga cho daijnai*Ajópedé*.” Enga ore chedu ujetiga ore chujé. Chi ore déji yoquiquigade*(dialecto diferente: queyiquigade).*Jé*Ajópedái*chiraja uje ore déji yoquiquigade enga ore chujé yoque dirome. Mu chaca*Ajódedái*iji ta. Chajna cutérone iji degújnai*(el campamento que tenía ayer)*. Chicai te. E yajnángo dajei. Jé chaca. Chicai te. Mu yico degúi enga yicoi doridie.
english: But U*ayé*was lying. She was a liar. We returned because she was lying about the fight going on. And she said: “*Ajópedé*bawled you guys out, and now they are turning on*Ajópedái*and about to come to blows.

ayoreo: Jesuse chiroque danguresõripisi aja Saulo uje i u Ananías ujetiga chiqueta enga chimopo dajiritígo rĩ. Jesuse chósi tome Ananías: “Bo ga abágui uje yajegáite Sáulo uté ujetiga jno yujnomei údi aja purása quedéjnane.”*(Hechos 9:15 aja 18)*
english: Jesus told him, “Go into their city and then I will tell you what I want you to do.”

ayoreo: “*Cucha Urõsódatéi á*, a angureta jóroque ua ome yajnatígo, yatígo ore to durasedie. ¿Que ajna gójni a? babárique iji ore. Urénga cuchiyíode jõrai tupúsu dajeque toguéodedatéi, dajeque tuguréodedatéi, yajnamenie ore dai tuqué,*Dejabi*uqué gajneri. Yacãra agoyé uneréjnaja tudaque ome*Cucha Urõsódatéi*uqué.
english: *Oh, you who caused such awful harm*, in vain was your confidence in being able to kill my grandsons and my sons. How is it that you haven’t, as you planned, been able to take one of them as your victim?

ayoreo:
Chi yocangaráni tu dirome, Jesus
iji deréjnai uñeque. Ujetiga yi que déji to que, ga yujnúsi jórogui. Yujnúsi
jórogui Dupade ucuei casé. Que toque ujetiga qué*tiémporaque*déji jetiga qué yangureta.
english: One day Jesus will show himself as Lord. If my name is not there in the book, I will be sad, but in vain, it will not change anything. I’ll be sad that God looked for it, but it wasn’t there. There will be no time left for me to believe.

ayoreo: Ore chojninga: “¿Cuchárique guejnaque báningo?” Ga chojninga: “Gajnoque Quedéjna chejna yayode ore.”
english: They say he ran off and climbed a **chúrume*i tree. (A small tree with thorns.) And he said: “This kind of tree (with thorns) is not found by the river.” They say he slept in the branches of the ‘*chúrumei’*tree all night long. At daylight he left.

ayoreo:
Jecute yicatecã́ri ua iji
gajnerĩ ujetiga arodipise Dugúidé ga asi ome pijóningai tome, yoque*Dupade*.
english: *Yoqui-Dupade*, I am talking to you for Dugúidé’s countrymen who are working. Watch over Dugúidé that he might seek you, and return to you, and serve you also. I know he will not refuse to let us work, but he was also chosen by you to help us as we both work and teach your Word.

ayoreo: Mu eca. Mu quigade diríca ga yingo garósi*Dupade Uruode*aja ore mu chojninga: “A jec ũré*Dupade*cuse jõ. Uje cuchárique chujerase yu,” chojninga, “Ga yitecurásu.” Tecu iji jnumone.
english: I said, “May neither of you suffer for your unbelief later on.”

ayoreo: Mu Dupade timíninga icatecarãitigade aja ajidedie. Ujetiga jé ũré gosi ome jeta doi Dupade ojningane cucha, ga Dupade chisi ome gosi ipotigádode cucha ome. Jé basa gosi te enga a gosi todo aníningo, daguidé áimeringui ome Dupade uruode ga que chutai Dupade iji cuchárique. Que chutai iparáta. Que yutai jeta chisi yibotígo jeta chisi ome yiguidedie ome yu, mu Dupade chimo uje udé chijna iyoite ga, jé a chojninga: “A yisi ome jne,” chojninga. Jé basa gosi a doi Dupade iroquéode mu que gaidi u ujetiga doi Dupade iroquéode, mu gaidi u ujetiga Dupade chisi dagúcharigo ome. Enga Dupade chojninga: “Ise ga yisi ome ipotiguéode cucha enga a jnorase dádi,” chojninga. “Ise ga yisi ome gaidode cucha enga chãra re ome yu,” chojninga.
english: *Dupade*says, a person’s prayers could be likened to a sharp knife. For you know that if someone prays for*Dupade*to give some beautiful thing to him,*Dupade*might say, “No, I think if I give it to you, you’ll be too pleased with it,” he will say. And*Dupade*is like a man whose children have knives. Their mothers don’t want them to have knives, because they don’t yet know how to use them. I don’t believe there is a mother anywhere that hasn’t warned her child about sneaking a knife!

ayoreo:
Yoqui-Dupade, yacaranguipis ome
ua iji gajnerĩ uje jé ijõre yoguiji Tobité uje yoquibosode ocoi iji Tobité.
Chamáñaje utagóji ua gu, Yoqui-Dupade. Chamáñaje ingongo yóguiji ua gatuaque
jetiga arodipise yóguiji guidai. Que gusu iji ne gajnerĩ uje yingo yoc aja ua,
Yoqui-Dupade, jé u iji dirode uje qué chicáji yoquíode, sequeredie uje qué
chicáji yoquiquéode. Que yirajágo baríringárique gu. Jecute qué yingongo yógaja
ua iji dirode jnese.
english: *Yoqui*–*Dupade*, I’m thankful to you right now that we are doing well here in Tobité and that our food is plentiful. That’s because we asked you for it,*Yoqui*–*Dupade*. That’s because we often come before you and ask you to take care of us here in our village. Not only right now am I bringing us before you,*Yoqui*–*Dupade*, but I will do it in the days and years ahead.

ayoreo:
Guiguíjnarique
uje yamátago ñane aja ujetiga yangácoi*Dupade**Uruode*iji, enga*Dupade*iguíjnai tu uté. Mu*Dupade*iguíjnapísi u danguresórone uyoque jnese enga chojningame yoque*Dupa uecai Iguíjnai u ore.*
english: **No Ayoré person ever refused to obey the bird *Asojna long ago:**Was there ever an Ayoré person anywhere, when we still believed in Asojna, that abandoned her ways and refused to obey her? No, everyone did what she said to do. When Asojna said: ‘Don’t drink water,’ they didn’t drink water. When she said: ‘Don’t suck honey,’ they didn’t suck honey. When she said: ‘When they arrive back to camp after collecting honey, before they can drink water or eat honey they must go through the ceremonial whipping,’ and only after that was completed did they drink water and eat honey.

ayoreo: Ga chi ore doi*Tináiné*, ga*Tináiné*chijna re aja jogadi, ore ijnanguiéni, ga chojninga: ‘Déji de uacajnanguiéni.’
english: And when they brought Tináiné, Tináiné would take himself right to where the lost thing was.

ayoreo:
Que yimopo iji dejaque gadiógode
udojo rĩ iji ore viaje yui casé to rĩ ome jne. Ujetiga Satanase chajẽ
chirote yu gu.
english: The earth was not good for me at that moment. I wondered what bad thing my daughters might have done? And I had been looking forward to the word’s of Dupade so that nothing of Satan could reach me. But here he was with me.

ayoreo: E ico ga ibácoi ta. Enga icopo rĩ ga yocatigai uné. Ga e yicoi cuchade iji dati, chicorone. Mu imo cutẽri iji ebédua enga*Garáijnaidái*chijno. Ga yipéco.
english: And then we left and slept again. At daylight we left again and*what’s-his-name*said,*Pongora dáyabí*said: “Come on, we’ll stop later. And then the next time we stop, we’ll eat.”

ayoreo: *(Oidábiadé*cho quique: Mu que ore chiraja nanique uje a Satanas utocaidode u nanique, uje ore chiroque yocáquiode ore aja dáringaringo pocaningai gaisóño.)
english: But some, even thought they didn’t breath in and out after eating*ají*, proved to be greater warriors than some who did suck their breath in and out, because when they went into battle their enemies couldn’t overcome them.

ayoreo:
Jé yajéo gu, uje ise mu ca tague
yu go ome yadode udojo. E gusu udojo yadode uje yicárasiji Santa Cruz to
nanique. Anire, ise mu ca tague yu go uje yabayode gare. Yiya nanique gu uje
anire pota gapudíe jiedie. Yojninga: ‘Yiya to jne.’ Ga yijína Jnani ga e gusu.
Que toque jeta yijína jnacare uñeque.
english: That’s what I wanted to do, and I don’t think I lied about myself or about what I did. The only thing I did was to go to Santa Cruz. And I think I’ve not lied about having had only two husbands. I only left my first husband because he’d been unfaithful with many young women. And I told myself, ‘I will leave him.’ And then, I went to*Jnani*and only him. There is no way I would go to any other man.

ayoreo:
Mu que yãra uaque go.
Yacaranguipis ome Dupade uje yangureta Jesucristo to ga que yãra uaque go ome
yanguretaretigai. Yiraja uje Espíritu Sánto déji yu. Yipésupú ga ũrepíse uaque
to ome uacanguretaretigai ome Dupade Abi Jesucristo enga jé edúyo
uacanguretigai ome uté.  E gusu.
english: Let’s say I was that person. I’ll use myself as an example. If Jesus looked at the book and my name was not there, I would say to Him: “But I’ve taught people about you, I taught my fellow-ayoréos.”

ayoreo: Ore chajo údi.
english: Another said: “I’ll think about her, concentrate on her, and thoughts of her will always be before me! She’ll be my wife!”

ayoreo:
Ga chi chojninga: “Qué yagábu gajnerí.”
Chisa a. Chétingãre mu uneréjna. Mu chi chojningame dabai, yocai jnani bajai,
“Boji ti. Agu to á,” chojninga, “Enga jec ũré uneréjna. A Dupade chãra yoque,”
chojninga, “Uje chãraque diríca,” chojninga.
english: She said: “Here they are. And I am going to eat them.” But then she said: “But if we eat this fruit, we’ll die just like*Dupade*said.”

ayoreo: Jecucha ore naráraja maquina mu que yicháji yuruode aja to go iji de máquina iji iguíjnai iji Dupade iguíjnai. Mu yicháji to iji de. Yiraja yu gu ga jé yicháji to yuruode. Anire, jecucha yojninga: “¿Jé gotique gu a uje que yicháji yurúrigo to?”
english: People are always recording their words, but I never have until now put my words on the machine here in God’s house. Right now, I’m putting them on it. I am sure of my faith and that is why I’m doing it. I’m always telling myself: “Why is it I never have told about my faith and put it on the machine?”

ayoreo:
Jé ũrepise
uje yugúcoi Tobité te ga udúco Dupade anguresórone ujnoméone uje ore changureta
Dupade iji Rincón, jéoga Zapoco, enga
unere ome yoque. Uje udúco Poza Verde gosode ujnomei uje ore cho jnome
Dupade Uruode ga yoco yocáyugu.
Ga jé u San Pablo ojningane ome yoque uje chojninga: “Uhuaque uje yiroque
Tíquico aja uaque jeta angáchoi yocujnoméone, angáchoi iji pijnani gajnerĩ.”
english: I pray to you in your son Jesus Christ’s name. Amen

ayoreo: – ore uniri tu*G**uerácadie*, ga jiei ore to.
english: *Cuterepajógosode*or*Cuterepaogorónegosode –*“People of honey” (called by two names) Their land was referred to as*‘guerácadie’*; there were many*Cutérepajógosode*.

ayoreo: *Dijaide – Cochabamba, Bolivia – 1970**Marina de Morarie churúsãre Dijaide acajnaquéode*
english: That is all I have to say tonight, but let’s never forget*Dupade*when we are together, whether*Ayoreos or Cojñone*, all of us who believe in Him – let’s keep our minds upon Him all the time, remembering to pray. That is all I have to say.

ayoreo: Chi ca chatata go,*Dachagaidé*. Que chatata dei go. Chi ore po, dajoguenie, dai ejode. Ijoguenie chi ore po ejode.
english: The oldest son said: “Probably*Dachagaidé*wanted us to think the grave was somewhere else.” He said, “This place is too neat with it’s mound. This is probably where he buried him, that’s why he worked so hard to camouflage it.*Dachagaidé*thought he’d found a place that wouldn’t be discovered. And it’s right here by the road,” he said, “Surely this is it.”

ayoreo:
Jesus chojninga:
“Que iraja yiríringárique dire, mu a yiyague uacamánique,” chojninga.
english: Jesus said: “I don’t know when I’ll return, but suddenly I’ll be there!” That’s what he said.

ayoreo: *Oidabiadé – Campo Loro, Paraguay – 1988.*
english: He was inside the house with his companions, planning how to kill*Dachapiboraté*. He said: “I’ll kill that witch doctor*Dachapiboraté*who eats from his garden.”

ayoreo:
Yoque Dupade, yacaranguipis ome
ua uje barote yóguiji erámone diríca iji yocajesúode erámio. Yugúcopóji de rĩ
jnese, jnanione ore uyoque. Yoque Dupade, jeta goto déji siñeque, jetiga cáma
unere baruode yui tome to gajneri, ga asíome gajneri baruode, ga unere yui ome
to gajneri, yoque Dupade. Gusu ua uje baruode cho cucha uneréjnane ome yoque,
Dupade.
english: Our Dupade, we pray to you today at our Friday meeting. I thank you, and we are happy that we are going to hear your Word today. Our Dupade, I am not ashamed to share your Word with my companions who are true believers. I am not ashamed of your Word even when others laugh at us.

ayoreo: Chi ore chujé ga chi Chiquenone ore chujé bajaque. Chi ore chijna dajéode aja guesi tiji gai. Ga chi ayoré dacasutedie ipesungórone u udojo, ome*Jaboidé*. Chiquenone chi ore chijna dajéode aja guesi ga ore cho je-je-je iji gai.
english: Once long ago, the Ayoreos ingested red hot peppers, and it made them so anger inside  that they ended up killing a very ugly official, according to*Jaboidé*.

ayoreo:
Enga arajáyo gotique, yujodie
chequei uaque, uje yirasé yu, mu que yirasepise yu go. Arajayope gotique,
yujodie chequei, uje yirasé yu mu que yirasepise yu go. Ujeta jec ũré ga
arajáyo Dupade uruode. Yirasé yu gu anire, yirasé yu mu que yirasepise yu
gotique; a jé ca déji uaque to. Uje yirasé yu mu uje yangureta Dupade ome
yajéode. Mu aníre, u yabai uje cáma changureta Dupade to, ga u uje a que
yirasepise yu go. Uerate jetiga yejnángo yógaja Dupade Uruode – yabai, yabode uyoque.
english: I love it when my fellow women stand before us and give their testimonies. I don’t know if you who are my fellow believers enjoy this also, but I do. I like to hear the stories they tell about themselves. I’m also happy that we are increasing in number in our meetings.

ayoreo:
Jecucha yiya Dupade adode mu
yorase paraque diríca. Ore chojninga: “Urusãráquedé ga Dupade anguresõre ga
cho yoque to.” Yirajágo uje cucha omi déji gaté. Dupade ipesudi cucha uerate déji gaté. Jeta yo parágome Dupade adode, a yoco parágome Dupade, ga íjnoque Dupade ueratigai tiji yoc. Jé yocadi pitoringai jeta yoco parágome. Adi jé cho uje que yiraja cucháric. Que yirajapise cuchárigo. Jecute yingo uaque to yate bajade.
english: I would stop following*Dupade*and start making mistakes. People would say about me: “Urusãráquedé is a believer in Dupade, but he acts just like us.”

ayoreo: Jnacarode ore di ijibujaidasorane ga ore chucué dai. Mu ore chopise dai casica uje ore abujadie yorícojna uje umare. Ore peropíse dai. Ore daté chojninga: “Aguitóyo. Yijnájoi uacai casé.*Dachagaidé*jõcaratáguebei a chujé.”
english: He quickly buried his victim, to hide what he had done. He put sticks over the top of the grave to make it look less like a grave.

ayoreo: Chacãrapo dacajnaquéode aja cucha pojnánguenie rĩ ga chojningame dacadigode, darasade ore: “Cajicho to cucha pojnánguenie. Que origuidéraque cuse mu Dupade pésu ore ga ueradei ore. Ore iguidedie gajnui u ore úringai uerate.”
english: He also used flowers as examples and this is what he told his disciples and his countrymen: “Just look at the flowers. They don’t wear clothing to make them look beautiful; it’s just how*Dupade*made them. Their beauty is their clothing.”

ayoreo: Cho cuyuque dejai gatócoro mu ayorei chisa yu. Chojninga: “Yimóji bari jne.” Yo cuyuc mu ayorei chisa yu. Yajire mu yajei u ayoré quedéjna mu yiguios. Mu yojninga: “Bo, ajni to. Que yipota ua.”
english: It was the middle of the night when someone grabbed me. He said: “I’m going to sleep with you.” I awoke and someone grabbed me. I looked and thought it was a stranger but it was a relative. But I said: “Go away. I don’t want you.”

ayoreo: *Marina de Morarie churúsãre Cajoide atatagode udojo nanique.*
english: *Transcribed and translated to English by: Maxine Morarie.*

ayoreo:
Uje eduguéjna cuchábi Faraón chi
mo nanique iji dejai. Mu urigode gajnárecho. Enga chi chĩ́cara ga aquirájna
ueradedie doi. Mu quigade ga aquirájna godóguedíe chi doi dojodie aquirájna
urojnai enga chi chujé. Enga chi tagu ga jogadi chona.
english: King Pharaoh was sleeping one night. But he had a bad dream. He dreamed about some healthy cattle. But afterwards, some sickly, skinny cows attacked the fat cattle and killed them. And they devoured them and nothing of them was left. And the king was troubled. The dream he had was very ugly.

ayoreo: ¡Chi chise ode! Chajire mu jnup umare aja.
english: The oldest son said to the youngest: “You go to one of the villages. I’ll go to the other villages where our father was going when he left us. Surely someone has killed him,” that’s what he said to his younger brother.

ayoreo: Mu chojninga: “Jé bãrarare. Bojninga, ‘chaguhei chejna yu.’ Yácare ga yisi babotígo ome ua.” Chácare. Chisi*dutué*ome. Chácare ga e taque.*Adudui*jnui*Anta,*ga*Adudui*pose*dutué*cucha.*Anta*que dique to.
english: But*Dajusui*said, “Yes, you did. You said, ‘Hunger is going to finish me off! ¡Jõ*aaaa!”*

ayoreo:
Adi jé u ayorérique jeta chimo
Jesuse mu que changureta úringai Dupa, ga Dupade chejna uté áyipie ajirigode.
english: When he was nearing the city, Jesus appeared to him and caused him to become blind.

ayoreo: “¡E chijna!”
english: When a person is ‘touched’ they say,*He got him!*

ayoreo:
Yicatecã́ri
ua iji babi Jesucristo i. Amẽ.
english: You alone are over all things. Over death. Over Satan and his demons. As David’s father, Ecarai, teaches us right now, forgive our ugly ways and the sin inside us.

ayoreo:
Enga jé adi tu gajnerĩ ujetiga ore irasique baruode
ujetiga ore tagu. Jecute yicatecã́ri ua gajnerĩ ujetiga arodipise yoque
gajnerĩ iji ne, Rincóngosode uyoque. Ujetiga átaja yoque gajnerĩ enga asíome
yoque pijoningai tome yoque gajnerĩ, yoque Dupade. Asíome petotiguei tome
yoque, gajnerĩ, yoque Dupade. Asíome pijnoraquei tome yoque. Uje yicatecã́ri
ua ome Jesus i. Amẽ.
english: I know if you’re a young woman, you’re happy, too. You play around and mistakenly think that’s all there is to life. But there’s more to life when you decide to trust in*Dupade*. If you want*Dupade*’s state of being the only one you believe in, that becomes your life from then on.

ayoreo: Jnanione ore dayode u iyatade. Iyatade ore narare ore gai uje ore chajna guesi iji taningapísi. Ore chojninga: “Ãrijíño ga ajnáño uaté aja guesi ga amácho to jne,  ga yoco paraque to jne.”
english: The men who make preparations for the Bird Festival are called ‘*Iyatade’;*only grown men and their fathers participate in being ‘*Iyatade.’*It’s the “Iyatade” that speak over the young men participants and get them going in the very beginning of the Festival. They say: “Wake up! Follow her! Chase after her! And when you do it, we’ll be very quiet.”

ayoreo: Uje gosi angureni tu Dupade ome dacuecaringuei. Jé basa uñeque a doi Dupade Uruode ga, gosi changureta dajmainie ome datrabajádoringáne jeta chise dabosode. Jé basa ga gosi changureta dabijnani, danguretaretigai ome Dupade adode uje chingo Dupade Uruode aja yóguiji erámi. Enga uné gosi u uje sue yoquibai. Enga uné gosi tu uje sue dojode ayoréode, cojñoque yoquibai jnese iji erámi.
english: The only thing Paul cared about in those days was his faith in*Dupade*. That’s why*Dupade’s Words*tell us not to give in to our fleshly desires if we are true believers. I’m always telling us that our human side is always trying to get the best of our spiritual side. But let’s surrender our will to*Dupade*’s will and let*Dupade*have the use of our bodies while we are in this world.*(Romans 12:1)*

ayoreo:
Mu Dupade
chimo icaniratiguei, chimo úringai paraque, chimo úringai anire osongáca. Ga
Dupade chojninga: “Udute bacatecãraitigade. Mu a cáma atoi jne. A qué yõrape
sequeredie tiji bei te jne. Ga sequeredie a qué chejnape yirídai jne iji bei
iji batoringai yui.” Ga unojo jéta Dupade chimesẽre yoque gosi, ga Dupade
aníri qué chisi ome yoquijóningai garósi ome yoque jeta jé yicabi Uruode gaté.
english: **Ephesus****to encourage believers****:***(Ephesians 6:21,22)*

ayoreo: –ore uniri tu*C**ucójnai*.
english: There were large*cucoi*trees in their land, but they left there and began to live in the ‘*jachai*’  and in the plains. Their chiefs were:*Jonanine,*and*Acui*.

ayoreo:
Gusu yacajnaquéode iji durase udaque to ome yoque. Enga yacaranguipise
ome uje yijorane ore cho paraque iji yacajnaquéode udé údi. Enga gusu
yacajnaquéode.
english: “Have faith in me,” God says, “Be true and faithful to me, and if someone speaks ill of you, don’t turn around and speak ill of him, just be still,” God says. “Just trust in me and believe in me. Their future is already planned, they will perish at the end of time.” And he says. “I will deny those who deny me and continue in sin.”

ayoreo: Chojninga: “Yajire jeta yipogai ome yodi. A ca yóji jeta yipogai ome.” Mu chóji yodi ga unéri ga chacãre yodi gai. Chojninga: “Yacaranguipis uje que yipogája to. A yóji jne.”
english: *(**Samané comments**: There isn’t any ‘jutai’ here. It’s in other places. It’s a kind of honey that pouts. It’s just like the ‘abéjadíe.’  Violeta (Joyce), you would like it. You would say, this is really good.)*

ayoreo: *Chequé Dago*ajéode jno yúdi aja darásade ga ore urejanie ore chicha yu aja darásade uje jnacare pariguéjna yu. Que yijína aja urásade. Deroco yugúchade dacaranone yui jeta yijína*Chequé Dago*.
english: But*Cheque Dago*ignored me and she slept with me.

ayoreo: “Be yibai ga yijnoque ua,” chojninga. E jno údi. Jno údi ga jé adi dasocai que uje pojna erámi.
english: “But you don’t have any food,”*Adudui*said.

ayoreo:
Mu peroi chi chojninga: “Juan,
Pedro, uacamóño yu to nanique uje ibasíji ité nanique ga noñaque yu aja guesi
nanique. Enga qué yugúsiji de iji dirode unojo. Ga asíyope yu iji uacabotique,
jeoga uacapláta ajóningárique. Uacamóño to uje peroque yu nanique iji ité ajei
ga jé qué u aja dirode udojo.”
english: Joseph said: “I understand. I know the meaning of your dream. It means that your master will let you serve him again, and you will bring him wine to drink. But I didn’t know what your dream meant, it was*Dupade*’s knowledge that he gave to me. That is how I knew the meaning of your dream.” This is what Joseph told him.

ayoreo:
Adi jé u ome yoque, disi ejode
uyoque. Uje Jesuse ore chécãre yoqui to, enga yoqui tu Cristiano. Dupade
anguresórone ore i u Cristiano. Ujetiga apota angureta Jesuse ga angureta
Jesuse. Jesuse yui guesája ome ua. Que parique Jesuse yui. Jesus chojninga:
“Abágui yu ujetiga apota yu. Yiguíjnai piagoi guesája, mu baguíjnai u uje
piagoi parígome yu,” chojninga. Jesus ojningai yoquiguíjnai
piagoi, que yoquiguíjnapísi u go, yocáyipie piagoi u Jesus ojningai. Ujeta
apota Jesuse ga achẽre báyipie piagoi gai ome, ga Jesuse chacája gai, ga
